Fecal Contamination in Sacral Pressure Injuries: A Practice-Focused Review

Érico Henrique de Lima Araújo, RN, MSc
Rivelino Verdier Dantas Montenegro, PhD

Abstract

Background: Sacrococcygeal pressure injuries occur in a microclimate-prone anatomical area adjacent to fecal efflux. Fecal contamination is frequently managed reactively, yet it functions as a distinct and potentially modifiable driver of dressing failure and care burden.

Hypothesis / Aim: We hypothesised that fecal contamination represents an under-addressed pathway to dressing disruption and sought to synthesise mechanisms, practice gaps and comparative isolation approaches to inform practice and research.

Methods: This practice-focused narrative review prioritised adult-focused literature on fecal incontinence, sacral/perineal contamination, IAD/MASD, dressing failure related to soiling/microclimate, microbiome/biofilm, and fecal isolation strategies. Searches were conducted in major biomedical sources (e.g., PubMed/Medline and Google Scholar), prioritising 2020-2025 publications and including seminal earlier economic literature when relevant. Selection prioritised higher-level evidence (systematic reviews, RCTs, guidelines) with contextual inclusion of mechanistic and practice reports. Quality appraisal was pragmatic (evidence hierarchy and risk-of-bias considerations as reported). Data were extracted and synthesised qualitatively around mechanisms, failure modes, comparative approaches and practice implications.

Results: Conventional containment and barrier products reduce skin exposure but do not reliably block natal cleft “tracking” toward the coccyx and dressing borders. Recurrent contamination precipitates premature dressing loss, repeated manipulation and workload amplification; it may also increase bioburden and biofilm risk. Invasive fecal management systems can reduce exposure in selected settings but have contraindications and fit limitations.

Conclusions: Fecal contamination should be treated as a distinct, modifiable risk factor in sacral pressure injury care. Proactive isolation strategies and structured escalation pathways may preserve dressing dwell time and reduce unplanned re-dressing events, warranting further evaluation.

Keywords: Biofilm; Fecal incontinence; Incontinence-associated dermatitis; Pressure injury; Sacral region; Wound dressing

Contents

  • Key Messages
  • Introduction
  • Methods
  • Results
  • Discussion
  • Conclusion
  • Implications for Clinical Practice
  • Further Research
  • References

Key Messages

  • A. Overview: Fecal contamination of sacral dressings is a high-impact, modifiable driver of dressing failure and care inefficiency in pressure injury management.
  • B. Aim: To synthesise mechanisms, practice gaps and fecal isolation strategies to support proactive prevention of contamination and define a pragmatic research agenda.
  • C. Key findings: Diapers and barrier products reduce skin exposure but do not mechanically block natal cleft “tracking”; invasive fecal management systems may help selected patients but are not universally applicable—supporting evaluation of non-invasive external barrier strategies.

Introduction

Sacrococcygeal pressure injuries (PIs) occur at the intersection of pressure, shear, friction and a hostile microclimate. In clinical practice, the sacral region is frequently exposed to occlusion from absorbent products, heat accumulation, perspiration and moisture, with additional challenges in people who are immobile, frail, or dependent for personal care. When fecal incontinence is present, the local environment can be repeatedly contaminated and cleaned, generating an unplanned cycle of “soil-clean-re-dress” that disrupts dressing dwell time and increases workload.

A characteristic failure mode is fecal “tracking” along the natal (intergluteal) cleft. Absorbent products are designed primarily for containment and absorption; they do not mechanically isolate the cleft pathway. In a bedridden patient, liquid or semi-formed stool may travel along this channel toward the coccyx and dressing border, contaminating even well-selected dressings shortly after application.

This review synthesises: (1) mechanisms by which fecal exposure destabilises the sacral wound environment, (2) why commonly used measures may be insufficient to prevent contamination-driven dressing loss, (3) comparative strategies for fecal isolation (including invasive and non-invasive options), and (4) clinical implications and a research agenda that treats contamination events as measurable operational outcomes.

Methods

This is a practice-focused narrative review. Eligibility criteria prioritised adult-focused publications addressing: fecal incontinence in immobile/dependent adults; sacral and perineal wound contamination; incontinence-associated dermatitis (IAD) and moisture-associated skin damage (MASD); dressing failure associated with soiling and microclimate; microbial burden and biofilm concepts relevant to chronic wounds; and strategies to isolate fecal effluent from sacral dressings.

Searches were conducted in major biomedical sources (e.g., PubMed/Medline and Google Scholar), prioritising 2020-2025 publications. Seminal older health-economic sources were included when frequently cited as context for pressure-injury cost and workforce impact. [10]

Study selection prioritised higher-level evidence (systematic reviews, randomised trials, consensus documents and guidelines) with contextual inclusion of mechanistic and practice reports when needed to clarify anatomical pathways and failure modes. Quality appraisal was pragmatic: evidence hierarchy was prioritised, and reported risk-of-bias limitations were considered. Findings were synthesised qualitatively into clinically actionable themes.

Results

1) Mechanisms: microclimate disruption, chemical irritation, and the dressing-skin interface

The sacral region is vulnerable to microclimate instability because it is subject to occlusion, local heat, perspiration and friction. Fecal effluent adds moisture loading and irritant exposure that can compromise the stratum corneum and increase vulnerability to MASD/IAD. [1] In practice, repeated cleansing and friction can strip protective lipids and exacerbate barrier disruption. This has direct relevance to dressing performance: compromised periwound skin and persistent moisture undermine adhesive borders, promote edge lift, and shorten intended dwell time.

Fecal material can also influence pH and contains enzymatic components (e.g., proteases, lipases and bile salts) that are plausibly irritant to skin and periwound tissues. While the exact contribution of each chemical component varies by stool consistency and patient factors, the clinical pattern is recognisable: maceration, erythema, superficial erosion and discomfort in the perineal and sacral area, often coexisting with pressure-related tissue damage. Importantly, this is not solely a “skin” problem. Once the dressing border fails, contamination may reach the wound bed, and repeated dressing removal can mechanically traumatise fragile granulation tissue and wound edges.

2) Microbial burden and biofilm relevance in the context of repeated contamination

Pressure injuries are frequently polymicrobial, and contemporary discussions emphasise the role of microbial communities and biofilm in chronicity and delayed healing. [2] [3] Fecal exposure increases bioburden and introduces a complex gut-derived microbial load. Even without clinically evident infection, repeated contamination events may increase the probability of critical colonisation, odour, exudate changes and inflammatory burden. Biofilm is particularly relevant to practice because it can reduce the apparent effectiveness of topical antimicrobials and necessitate repeated debridement and dressing changes. [3] While this review does not assert that fecal exposure directly “causes” biofilm, it supports the practical inference that reducing contamination frequency is a plausible step to stabilise the wound environment and preserve the value of advanced dressings.

3) The natal cleft “tracking” pathway as a discrete failure mode

A central practice gap is the anatomical pathway that enables stool tracking. The natal cleft forms a channel that can direct loose stool rostrally toward the coccyx. Absorbent products, even when well fitted, do not reliably “obliterate” this space. The clinical consequence is a predictable failure mode: the dressing border becomes contaminated and loses integrity, forcing unplanned replacement. This failure mode is operationally important because it is measurable: contamination events affecting the dressing and dressing changes attributable to contamination can be captured as endpoints, providing a bridge between clinical rationale and service-level impact.

4) Comparative strategies to isolate fecal effluent from the sacral region

4.1 Containment and barrier products

Diapers and absorbent pads, combined with structured cleansing and skin protection routines (barrier creams/films), remain foundational for hygiene and intact-skin protection. They reduce exposure duration and can reduce irritant contact on intact skin. However, they do not reliably prevent tracking to the dressing border and do not provide mechanical isolation of an open wound. Additionally, barrier products may be displaced by repeated cleansing and friction. [1]

4.2 Prophylactic sacral dressings

High-quality evidence supports multilayer silicone-adhesive foam dressings to prevent sacral/coccygeal pressure ulcers in at-risk patients, including populations with severe diarrhoea or fragile skin. [5] [6] These dressings likely act by reducing friction/shear, supporting microclimate management and distributing local stress. Economic evaluations suggest that prophylactic dressings can be cost-saving when targeted to high-risk patients. [7] However, prophylactic dressings alone may still fail when stool directly contaminates the border or undermines adhesion.

4.3 Invasive fecal management systems (FMS)

Fecal management systems (rectal devices) can reduce exposure and may decrease workload in selected intensive care contexts. Contemporary reviews and consensus statements emphasise that FMS can be clinically useful but must be applied selectively due to invasiveness, contraindications, patient tolerability and care-setting fit. [8] [9]

4.4 Diversion/colostomy

Surgical diversion provides high-level isolation for selected complex cases but is invasive, resource intensive and not a scalable response for routine contamination prevention.

4.5 Non-invasive external anatomical barrier strategies

The above comparators highlight a clinical niche: strategies that (i) mechanically interrupt the natal cleft tracking pathway, (ii) are non-invasive and compatible with diaper-based workflows, and (iii) can be applied in settings where FMS is unsuitable. External anatomical barriers aim to create a physical interface between the perineal effluent and the sacral dressing border. Although device-specific evidence is limited and should be generated prospectively, the concept is coherent with the mechanism of failure (tracking) and the operational endpoint (contamination-driven dressing replacement). Evidence supporting physical “guard” approaches in other contexts illustrates plausibility. [4]

Discussion

This review supports reframing fecal contamination as a modifiable driver of sacral dressing failure and service-level inefficiency. The key insight is not that current care is “wrong”, but that routine containment and barrier strategies were not designed to mechanically isolate the natal cleft pathway. When the pathway is not controlled, a predictable failure mode persists: unplanned dressing replacement after contamination events. This is clinically relevant because it disrupts intended dressing dwell time and can aggravate fragile periwound tissues. It is operationally relevant because it consumes nursing time, increases supply use and may contribute to delayed stabilisation of wounds. [10]

A European “practice pragmatism” lens emphasises that solutions must fit diverse settings (acute care, rehabilitation, long-term care, community) and must respect patient dignity while minimising invasiveness. Therefore, escalation pathways should consider the least invasive effective option first, guided by stool consistency, frequency of soiling, patient tolerance and contraindications. In selected ICU patients, FMS may be appropriate. [8] [9]

Importantly, the evidence base should be strengthened using endpoints that reflect the problem. For contamination-driven dressing failure, actionable endpoints include: (i) number of contamination events affecting the dressing border, (ii) dressing changes attributable to contamination, (iii) dressing dwell time, (iv) periwound skin integrity (IAD/MASD), and (v) workforce impact (time spent on unplanned cleaning and re-dressing). These endpoints also lend themselves to pragmatic designs feasible in busy clinical environments.

Limitations of this review include its narrative scope and qualitative synthesis, as well as the heterogeneity of settings and comparator strategies. The review does not claim that fecal exposure directly causes infection or biofilm in all cases; rather, it supports the practical inference that repeated contamination increases risk of destabilising the wound environment and increasing care burden. Future controlled studies are needed to quantify the incremental benefit of isolation strategies beyond prophylactic dressings and structured skin care.

Conclusion

Fecal contamination of sacral dressings is an under-specified yet high-impact contributor to premature dressing loss and care inefficiency. The natal cleft tracking pathway helps explain why standard containment and barrier approaches may be insufficient in patients with recurrent fecal soiling. Evidence supports prophylactic sacral dressings for PI prevention, and invasive fecal management systems can be useful in selected contexts; however, neither strategy universally addresses contamination-driven dressing disruption. [5] [6] [8] [9] A structured escalation pathway and prospective evaluation of non-invasive external anatomical barrier strategies are warranted, using objective endpoints that capture contamination events and dressing dwell time.

Implications for Clinical Practice

  • Document fecal contamination as a distinct, measurable cause of dressing failure (not merely “soiling”) to quantify its operational impact.
  • Use a structured escalation pathway for recurrent contamination: optimise microclimate management and cleansing technique, confirm dressing selection/border protection, and consider fecal isolation options aligned to setting and contraindications.
  • Recognise that reducing unplanned dressing replacement may meaningfully reduce nursing workload, a major cost driver in PI care. [10]

Further Research

  • Prospective studies should quantify contamination events affecting dressings, dressing changes attributable to contamination, dressing dwell time, periwound skin integrity (IAD/MASD) and nursing time/workflow impact.
  • Comparative effectiveness research is needed to define which patients benefit most from specific fecal isolation strategies across settings.
  • Implementation studies should evaluate feasibility, training needs, safety monitoring and economic impact using real-world endpoints.

References

  1. McNichol L, Ayello EA, Phearman LA, Pezzella PA, Culver EA. Moisture-associated skin damage: expanding practice based on the new consensus. J Wound Ostomy Continence Nurs. 2022;49(3):221–231.
  2. de Wert LA, Rensen SS, Soons Z, Poeze M, Bouvy ND, Penders J. The cutaneous microbiome in hospitalized patients with pressure ulcers. Sci Rep. 2020;10:5963.
  3. Kabir F, Yung DBY, Nizer WS, et al. Pressure injuries and biofilms: microbiome, model systems and therapies. Wound Repair Regen. 2025;33(1):In press.
  4. Lo WB, Herbert K, Rodrigues D, Afshari FT. The ‘transverse guard’ wound dressing technique to reduce faecal contamination after spinal surgery in neonates and infants. Br J Nurs. 2020;29(12):S12–S15.
  5. Oe M, Sasaki S, Shimura T, Takaki Y, Sanada H. Effects of multilayer silicone foam dressings for the prevention of pressure ulcers in high-risk patients: a randomized clinical trial. Adv Wound Care (New Rochelle). 2020;9(12):649–656.
  6. Forni C, et al. Effectiveness of a multi-layer silicone-adhesive polyurethane foam dressing as prevention for sacral pressure ulcers in at-risk in-patients: randomized controlled trial. Int J Nurs Stud. 2022;127:104172.
  7. Mezzalira E, Ambrosi E, Askew N, et al. Economic evaluation of multilayer silicone-adhesive polyurethane foam dressing for the prevention of pressure ulcers in at-risk hospitalized patients. Int J Health Policy Manag. 2024;13:In press.
  8. Gutting T, Pfützner A. The crucial role of fecal management systems in intensive care. Expert Rev Med Devices. 2024;21(8):701–707.
  9. Gutting T, et al. Recommendations for use of fecal management systems in intensive care units: results from an expert consensus meeting. Am J Infect Control. 2025;In press.
  10. Bennett G, Dealey C, Posnett J. The cost of pressure ulcers in the UK. Age Ageing. 2004;33(3):230–235.
